This is the same principle as writing a failing test first — you want to understand what goes wrong before adding complexity.

Test with diverse inputs. A prompt that works for one input may fail spectacularly on another. Build a test suite of 10-20 representative inputs including edge cases, long inputs, ambiguous inputs, and adversarial inputs. Track success rates across the suite.

Version your prompts. Treat prompts like code — store them in version control, document changes, and track performance metrics over time. When you change a prompt, run it against your test suite and compare results to the previous version.

Isolate variables. When iterating, change one thing at a time. If you change the system message, the few-shot examples, and the output format simultaneously, you cannot determine which change caused any improvement or regression.

Read the failures carefully. When a prompt produces bad output, analyze the failure mode. Is the model misunderstanding the task? Ignoring a constraint? Producing the wrong format? Each failure mode has a different fix. Misunderstanding suggests clearer instructions; ignoring constraints suggests stronger wording or moving them to the system message; wrong format suggests adding explicit examples.

Key Concept

Prompt engineering is empirical, not theoretical. The only way to know if a prompt works is to test it against real inputs. Build the habit of creating small evaluation datasets for every prompt-powered feature, and automate the testing process early. This is the AI equivalent of unit testing, and it pays for itself quickly as you iterate.

Key Takeaways

  • Every prompt has up to four components: instruction, context, input data, and output format. Explicitly separating these improves reliability.
  • System messages are your most powerful control lever — use them to set role, rules, and constraints that apply to every interaction.
  • Temperature controls randomness: use 0 for deterministic tasks (code, extraction) and 0.7–1.0 for creative tasks.
  • Few-shot prompting (providing examples) is one of the most effective ways to control model behavior, especially for format and classification tasks.
  • Structured prompts using delimiters, numbered steps, and XML tags prevent the model from confusing instructions with data.
  • Use JSON mode or structured outputs when your application needs to parse model responses programmatically.
  • Treat prompts like code: version them, test them against diverse inputs, and iterate systematically.

Practice Exercises

1. Write a prompt that takes a JavaScript function and returns a structured JSON review with fields for “bugs”, “performance_issues”, and “accessibility_concerns”. Test it with at least three different functions of varying complexity.

2. Create two versions of a sentiment classifier: one zero-shot and one few-shot with five examples. Test both on 10 ambiguous reviews (e.g., sarcastic, mixed sentiment) and compare accuracy. Document which cases each version handles better.

3. Build a PromptTemplate class that supports variable substitution, conditional sections (include a section only if a variable is provided), and automatic escaping of user input to prevent prompt injection. Test it with a code review template.

4. Experiment with temperature settings: send the same creative prompt (e.g., “Suggest a name for a developer productivity tool”) at temperatures 0, 0.5, 1.0, and 1.5, running each 5 times. Chart the diversity and quality of results to build intuition for the parameter.

Back to Table of Contents
Chapter 8

Advanced Prompting Techniques

From Frontend to AI

Think of advanced prompting techniques like design patterns in software engineering. Chain-of-thought prompting is like a pipeline pattern — data flows through sequential transformation stages. ReAct is like an event loop — the model cycles through reasoning, acting, and observing until the task is complete. Tree-of-thought is like a search algorithm — exploring multiple branches and pruning unsuccessful paths. You already use these architectural patterns; now you will apply them to guide how LLMs think.

Chain-of-Thought (CoT) Prompting

Chain-of-thought prompting is one of the most important breakthroughs in prompt engineering. The core insight is simple but powerful: when you ask a model to show its reasoning step by step before giving a final answer, accuracy on complex tasks improves dramatically. This works because LLMs generate text sequentially — each token is influenced by all previous tokens. When the model writes out intermediate reasoning steps, those steps become part of the context that informs the final answer.

Consider a concrete example. If you ask a model “If a store offers 20% off and then an additional 15% off the discounted price, what is the total discount?”, it might jump to an answer of 35%. But if you prompt it to reason step by step, it correctly computes: original price is 100, after 20% off it is 80, after 15% off 80 it is 68, so the total discount is 32%. The intermediate steps prevent the model from making a common shortcut error.

CoT is most valuable for tasks involving multi-step reasoning: mathematical word problems, logical deductions, code debugging, complex classification with multiple criteria, and planning tasks. For simple factual recall or straightforward formatting tasks, CoT adds unnecessary verbosity without improving accuracy.

PythonChain-of-thought prompting: explicit reasoning
# Without CoT - the model may jump to conclusions
naive_messages = [
    {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful assistant."},
    {
        "role": "user",
        "content": (
            "A web server receives 1,200 requests per minute. Each request "
            "takes 50ms to process. The server has 4 worker threads. "
            "Can the server handle the load without queuing?"
        )
    }
]

# With CoT - force step-by-step reasoning
cot_messages = [
    {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful assistant. Think through problems step by step."},
    {
        "role": "user",
        "content": (
            "A web server receives 1,200 requests per minute. Each request "
            "takes 50ms to process. The server has 4 worker threads. "
            "Can the server handle the load without queuing?\n\n"
            "Think through this step by step:\n"
            "1. Calculate requests per second\n"
            "2. Calculate how many requests one thread can handle per second\n"
            "3. Calculate total capacity with all threads\n"
            "4. Compare capacity to demand\n"
            "5. State your conclusion"
        )
    }
]

# CoT response will correctly work through:
# 1. 1200 req/min = 20 req/sec
# 2. One thread: 1000ms / 50ms = 20 req/sec per thread
# 3. 4 threads x 20 req/sec = 80 req/sec capacity
# 4. 20 req/sec demand < 80 req/sec capacity
# 5. Yes, with significant headroom (25% utilization)

Zero-Shot Chain-of-Thought

A remarkable discovery in prompt engineering was that simply appending “Let’s think step by step” to a prompt triggers chain-of-thought reasoning without providing any examples. This is called zero-shot CoT, and it is surprisingly effective across a wide range of tasks. Research by Kojima et al. (2022) showed that this simple phrase improved accuracy on math benchmarks by 10-15 percentage points.

Zero-shot CoT works because the phrase “Let’s think step by step” activates patterns the model learned during training — it has seen millions of step-by-step explanations and reasoning chains. The phrase effectively tells the model to engage its “careful reasoning” mode rather than its “quick answer” mode.

PythonZero-shot CoT with a practical debugging example
buggy_code = """
def merge_sorted_lists(list1, list2):
    result = []
    i, j = 0, 0
    while i < len(list1) and j < len(list2):
        if list1[i] <= list2[j]:
            result.append(list1[i])
            i += 1
        else:
            result.append(list2[j])
            j += 1
    return result
"""

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o",
    messages=[
        {
            "role": "system",
            "content": "You are an expert Python debugger."
        },
        {
            "role": "user",
            "content": f"This function has a bug. Find it and explain the fix.\n\n"
                       f"```python\n{buggy_code}\n```\n\n"
                       f"Let's think step by step."
        }
    ]
)

# The model will methodically trace through the code and identify
# that remaining elements in either list are not appended after
# the while loop exits, resulting in lost elements.
Key Concept

Chain-of-thought prompting works because it changes what the model attends to. Without CoT, the model generates an answer based on pattern matching to similar questions in its training data. With CoT, the model generates intermediate steps that become part of its context, allowing each subsequent step to build on verified intermediate results rather than jumping to a potentially incorrect conclusion.

ReAct: Reasoning + Acting

ReAct (Reasoning and Acting) is a prompting pattern that combines chain-of-thought reasoning with the ability to take actions and observe results. It was introduced by Yao et al. (2022) and has become the foundation for most LLM agent systems. The pattern follows a loop: the model produces a Thought (reasoning about what to do next), an Action (a specific operation to perform), and then processes an Observation (the result of that action) before continuing with the next thought.

ReAct is powerful because it combines the reasoning benefits of CoT with the ability to interact with the external world. A pure CoT approach is limited to what the model already knows; a ReAct agent can look up information, run calculations, query databases, and call APIs to fill in knowledge gaps.

For frontend developers, ReAct is conceptually similar to a middleware pipeline or an event loop: receive input, decide what to do, do it, observe the result, decide the next step. The loop continues until the task is complete or a stopping condition is reached.

PythonReAct pattern implementation
import json

tools = {
    "search_docs": lambda query: f"Documentation results for '{query}': ...",
    "run_query": lambda sql: f"Query returned 42 rows: [{{'id': 1, 'name': 'test'}}...]",
    "get_schema": lambda table: f"Table '{table}': id INT PK, name VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMP",
}

REACT_SYSTEM_PROMPT = """You are a helpful database assistant. You have access to these tools:

- search_docs(query): Search the documentation
- run_query(sql): Execute a SQL query and return results
- get_schema(table): Get the schema of a database table

For each step, use this exact format:

Thought: [Your reasoning about what to do next]
Action: [tool_name(arguments)]

After receiving an Observation, continue with another Thought/Action or provide your final answer:

Thought: [I now have enough information to answer]
Final Answer: [Your complete answer to the user's question]
"""

def run_react_agent(user_question: str, max_steps: int = 5) -> str:
    messages = [
        {"role": "system", "content": REACT_SYSTEM_PROMPT},
        {"role": "user", "content": user_question}
    ]

    for step in range(max_steps):
        response = client.chat.completions.create(
            model="gpt-4o",
            temperature=0,
            messages=messages
        )
        assistant_msg = response.choices[0].message.content
        messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": assistant_msg})

        # Check if the model has reached a final answer
        if "Final Answer:" in assistant_msg:
            return assistant_msg.split("Final Answer:")[-1].strip()

        # Parse and execute the action
        if "Action:" in assistant_msg:
            action_line = assistant_msg.split("Action:")[-1].strip().split("\n")[0]
            tool_name = action_line.split("(")[0].strip()
            tool_args = action_line.split("(", 1)[1].rstrip(")")

            if tool_name in tools:
                observation = tools[tool_name](tool_args)
            else:
                observation = f"Error: Unknown tool '{tool_name}'"

            messages.append({
                "role": "user",
                "content": f"Observation: {observation}"
            })

    return "Max steps reached without a final answer."

# Usage
answer = run_react_agent(
    "How many users were created in the last 7 days? "
    "First check the schema, then write and run the query."
)
ReAct Loop: Reasoning and Acting Pattern
The ReAct pattern alternates between Thought (reasoning), Action (tool use), and Observation (result processing). The loop continues until the model has enough information to produce a Final Answer. This example shows a two-step interaction where the agent first checks a database schema and then runs a query.

Self-Consistency: Multiple Reasoning Paths

Self-consistency is a technique that generates multiple independent reasoning paths for the same problem and then selects the most common answer. The intuition is straightforward: if you ask a model to solve a problem five times and it arrives at the same answer four times, that answer is more likely correct than if it gives a different answer each time.

To implement self-consistency, you set a higher temperature (e.g., 0.7) to encourage diverse reasoning paths, generate multiple completions for the same prompt, extract the final answer from each completion, and take the majority vote. This technique is especially effective for math, logic, and classification tasks where there is a single correct answer.

PythonSelf-consistency with majority voting
from collections import Counter

def self_consistent_answer(
    question: str,
    n_samples: int = 5,
    temperature: float = 0.7
) -> dict:
    """Generate multiple reasoning paths and take majority vote."""

    messages = [
        {
            "role": "system",
            "content": (
                "Solve the problem step by step. After your reasoning, "
                "write your final answer on the last line in the format: "
                "ANSWER: [your answer]"
            )
        },
        {"role": "user", "content": question}
    ]

    # Generate multiple independent responses
    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o",
        messages=messages,
        temperature=temperature,
        n=n_samples  # Request multiple completions at once
    )

    answers = []
    reasoning_paths = []
    for choice in response.choices:
        text = choice.message.content
        reasoning_paths.append(text)

        # Extract the final answer
        for line in reversed(text.split("\n")):
            if line.strip().startswith("ANSWER:"):
                answers.append(line.split("ANSWER:")[-1].strip())
                break

    # Majority vote
    vote_counts = Counter(answers)
    best_answer, best_count = vote_counts.most_common(1)[0]

    return {
        "answer": best_answer,
        "confidence": best_count / len(answers),
        "vote_distribution": dict(vote_counts),
        "num_paths": len(reasoning_paths)
    }

result = self_consistent_answer(
    "A store has 3 types of widgets. Type A costs $5, Type B costs $8, "
    "Type C costs $12. A customer buys 7 widgets for exactly $56. "
    "They buy at least one of each type. How many of each type did they buy?"
)
print(f"Answer: {result['answer']} (confidence: {result['confidence']:.0%})")

Tree-of-Thought: Exploring Multiple Branches

Tree-of-Thought (ToT) extends chain-of-thought by explicitly exploring multiple reasoning branches at each step, evaluating which branches are most promising, and pruning dead ends. While CoT follows a single linear reasoning path, ToT explores a tree of possibilities — similar to how a chess engine evaluates multiple moves ahead.

ToT is most useful for complex planning problems, creative tasks with many possible approaches, and puzzles where the correct path is not obvious from the start. It is more expensive than standard CoT (it requires multiple API calls per step) but can solve problems that linear reasoning cannot.

PythonTree-of-Thought: exploring multiple solution approaches
def tree_of_thought(problem: str, n_branches: int = 3, depth: int = 3) -> str:
    """Explore multiple reasoning branches and select the best path."""

    # Step 1: Generate multiple initial approaches
    branch_response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o",
        temperature=0.8,
        messages=[
            {
                "role": "system",
                "content": (
                    "You are a problem solver. Generate different approaches "
                    "to solve the given problem. For each approach, describe "
                    "the first step and assess its promise (high/medium/low)."
                )
            },
            {
                "role": "user",
                "content": (
                    f"Problem: {problem}\n\n"
                    f"Generate {n_branches} distinct approaches. For each:\n"
                    "1. Describe the approach in one sentence\n"
                    "2. Take the first step\n"
                    "3. Rate its promise: HIGH, MEDIUM, or LOW\n"
                    "4. Explain your rating"
                )
            }
        ]
    )

    branches = branch_response.choices[0].message.content

    # Step 2: Evaluate and select the most promising branch
    eval_response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o",
        temperature=0,
        messages=[
            {
                "role": "system",
                "content": "You are a critical evaluator of problem-solving approaches."
            },
            {
                "role": "user",
                "content": (
                    f"Problem: {problem}\n\n"
                    f"Proposed approaches:\n{branches}\n\n"
                    "Select the most promising approach and explain why. "
                    "Then solve the problem completely using that approach."
                )
            }
        ]
    )

    return eval_response.choices[0].message.content

Prompt Chaining: Sequential Multi-Step Workflows

Prompt chaining breaks a complex task into a sequence of simpler subtasks, where the output of one prompt becomes the input to the next. This is analogous to Unix pipes or middleware chains in web frameworks — each step in the chain has a focused responsibility, making the overall system easier to debug, test, and maintain.

Chaining is preferable to a single monolithic prompt for several reasons. First, each step can use a simpler, more focused prompt that is easier to optimize. Second, you can inspect intermediate results and add validation or error handling between steps. Third, different steps can use different models — a cheap, fast model for classification, a powerful model for generation.

PythonPrompt chaining: multi-step document processing pipeline
async def process_support_ticket(ticket_text: str) -> dict:
    """Process a support ticket through a chain of specialized prompts."""

    # Step 1: Classify the ticket
    classification = await client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",  # Fast model for classification
        temperature=0,
        response_format={"type": "json_object"},
        messages=[
            {
                "role": "system",
                "content": (
                    "Classify support tickets. Respond with JSON: "
                    '{"category": "bug|feature|question|billing", '
                    '"priority": "low|medium|high|critical", '
                    '"sentiment": "positive|neutral|negative"}'
                )
            },
            {"role": "user", "content": ticket_text}
        ]
    )
    ticket_meta = json.loads(classification.choices[0].message.content)

    # Step 2: Extract key details based on category
    extraction = await client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o",
        temperature=0,
        response_format={"type": "json_object"},
        messages=[
            {
                "role": "system",
                "content": (
                    f"Extract details from this {ticket_meta['category']} ticket. "
                    "Return JSON with: product_area, steps_to_reproduce (if bug), "
                    "affected_users (estimated scope), and key_phrases."
                )
            },
            {"role": "user", "content": ticket_text}
        ]
    )
    details = json.loads(extraction.choices[0].message.content)

    # Step 3: Generate a response draft (only for high priority)
    draft = None
    if ticket_meta["priority"] in ("high", "critical"):
        draft_response = await client.chat.completions.create(
            model="gpt-4o",
            temperature=0.3,
            messages=[
                {
                    "role": "system",
                    "content": (
                        "You are a senior support agent. Draft a response "
                        "that acknowledges the issue, provides immediate steps "
                        "if possible, and sets clear expectations for resolution. "
                        "Be empathetic but concise."
                    )
                },
                {
                    "role": "user",
                    "content": (
                        f"Ticket: {ticket_text}\n\n"
                        f"Category: {ticket_meta['category']}\n"
                        f"Priority: {ticket_meta['priority']}\n"
                        f"Details: {json.dumps(details)}"
                    )
                }
            ]
        )
        draft = draft_response.choices[0].message.content

    return {
        "classification": ticket_meta,
        "details": details,
        "draft_response": draft
    }

Role-Based Prompting

Assigning a specific persona or role to the model shapes its vocabulary, reasoning approach, and the type of knowledge it prioritizes. A model told “You are a security auditor” will focus on vulnerabilities and attack vectors; the same model told “You are a UX designer” will focus on usability and accessibility. Role-based prompting works because LLMs have learned the associations between roles and the kinds of content those roles produce.

Effective role-based prompts go beyond a simple title. The best personas include the role’s expertise level, years of experience, specific areas of focus, and communication style. “You are a senior backend engineer with 15 years of experience in distributed systems. You prioritize reliability and observability. You communicate concisely and always back claims with specific technical reasoning.”

A powerful variation is multi-persona prompting, where you ask the model to evaluate a problem from multiple perspectives. For example: “Evaluate this architecture proposal from three perspectives: (1) a security engineer focused on data protection, (2) a site reliability engineer focused on uptime and operability, and (3) a product manager focused on development velocity and feature delivery.”

PythonMulti-persona evaluation pattern
def multi_perspective_review(proposal: str, perspectives: list[dict]) -> dict:
    """Evaluate a proposal from multiple expert perspectives."""

    reviews = {}
    for persona in perspectives:
        response = client.chat.completions.create(
            model="gpt-4o",
            temperature=0.3,
            messages=[
                {
                    "role": "system",
                    "content": (
                        f"You are a {persona['role']} with {persona['experience']}. "
                        f"Focus on: {persona['focus']}. "
                        "Be specific and actionable in your feedback. "
                        "Rate the proposal: STRONG_APPROVE, APPROVE, NEUTRAL, "
                        "CONCERN, or STRONG_CONCERN."
                    )
                },
                {
                    "role": "user",
                    "content": f"Review this proposal:\n\n{proposal}"
                }
            ]
        )
        reviews[persona["role"]] = response.choices[0].message.content

    return reviews

# Usage
reviews = multi_perspective_review(
    proposal="We plan to store user sessions in Redis with no encryption...",
    perspectives=[
        {
            "role": "Security Engineer",
            "experience": "10 years in application security",
            "focus": "data protection, authentication, encryption"
        },
        {
            "role": "SRE",
            "experience": "8 years running distributed systems",
            "focus": "reliability, failover, monitoring, operational complexity"
        },
        {
            "role": "Tech Lead",
            "experience": "12 years in full-stack development",
            "focus": "maintainability, developer experience, technical debt"
        }
    ]
)

Retrieval-Augmented Prompting (RAG Preview)

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is a technique where you retrieve relevant information from an external knowledge base and inject it into the prompt before sending it to the model. This gives the model access to up-to-date, domain-specific information that may not exist in its training data. We will cover RAG in depth in Part V; here, we preview the core prompting pattern.

The key to effective retrieval-augmented prompting is how you present the retrieved context. Simply dumping a wall of text into the prompt is ineffective. Instead, clearly delineate retrieved content from instructions, indicate the source and relevance of each piece, and tell the model how to handle cases where the retrieved context does not contain the answer.

PythonRetrieval-augmented prompting pattern
def answer_with_context(question: str, retrieved_docs: list[dict]) -> str:
    """Answer a question using retrieved context documents."""

    context_str = "\n\n".join(
        f"<document source=\"{doc['source']}\" relevance=\"{doc['score']:.2f}\">\n"
        f"{doc['content']}\n"
        f"</document>"
        for doc in retrieved_docs
    )

    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o",
        temperature=0,
        messages=[
            {
                "role": "system",
                "content": (
                    "Answer questions using ONLY the provided context documents. "
                    "Rules:\n"
                    "1. If the context contains the answer, provide it with "
                    "   a citation like [Source: document_name]\n"
                    "2. If the context partially answers the question, say what "
                    "   you can answer and what is missing\n"
                    "3. If the context does not contain the answer, say "
                    "   'I don't have enough information to answer this question' "
                    "   - do NOT make up an answer\n"
                    "4. Never use knowledge outside the provided documents"
                )
            },
            {
                "role": "user",
                "content": (
                    f"<context>\n{context_str}\n</context>\n\n"
                    f"Question: {question}"
                )
            }
        ]
    )

    return response.choices[0].message.content

Meta-Prompting: Using LLMs to Improve Prompts

Meta-prompting is the practice of using an LLM to help you write, evaluate, and improve prompts. This creates a powerful feedback loop: you write an initial prompt, test it, then use the model to analyze failures and suggest improvements. This technique is valuable because models often have insight into what instructions they respond to best.

A practical meta-prompting workflow involves three stages. First, write your initial prompt and test it on a set of examples. Second, collect the failures — cases where the output was wrong, incomplete, or badly formatted. Third, present the original prompt, the failed examples, and the actual vs. expected outputs to the model, and ask it to suggest a revised prompt.

PythonMeta-prompting: automated prompt refinement
def refine_prompt(
    original_prompt: str,
    test_cases: list[dict],  # [{"input": ..., "expected": ..., "actual": ...}]
) -> str:
    """Use the model to suggest improvements to a failing prompt."""

    failures = [tc for tc in test_cases if tc["actual"] != tc["expected"]]

    if not failures:
        return original_prompt  # No failures to fix

    failure_report = "\n\n".join(
        f"Input: {f['input']}\n"
        f"Expected: {f['expected']}\n"
        f"Actual: {f['actual']}"
        for f in failures[:5]  # Limit to 5 examples
    )

    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o",
        temperature=0.3,
        messages=[
            {
                "role": "system",
                "content": (
                    "You are a prompt engineering expert. Analyze why a prompt "
                    "is failing and suggest a specific, improved version. "
                    "Focus on making minimal targeted changes rather than "
                    "rewriting from scratch."
                )
            },
            {
                "role": "user",
                "content": (
                    f"Original prompt:\n```\n{original_prompt}\n```\n\n"
                    f"Failure cases:\n{failure_report}\n\n"
                    "Analyze the failure pattern and provide an improved prompt. "
                    "Explain what you changed and why."
                )
            }
        ]
    )

    return response.choices[0].message.content
Pro Tip

When choosing between these advanced techniques, consider the trade-off between quality and cost. Chain-of-thought adds modest cost (longer outputs) for significant quality improvements on reasoning tasks. Self-consistency multiplies cost by the number of samples but delivers high-confidence answers. Tree-of-thought and prompt chaining are the most expensive but handle the most complex tasks. For most production applications, start with simple CoT, measure where it fails, and escalate to more expensive techniques only for those specific failure modes.

Key Takeaways

  • Chain-of-thought prompting dramatically improves reasoning accuracy by forcing the model to show intermediate steps. Use it for any task involving multi-step logic.
  • Zero-shot CoT (“Let’s think step by step”) is a surprisingly effective low-effort improvement for reasoning tasks.
  • ReAct combines reasoning with tool use, enabling models to interact with external systems in a thought-action-observation loop — the foundation of most AI agent architectures.
  • Self-consistency generates multiple reasoning paths and takes a majority vote, trading cost for confidence in the answer.
  • Prompt chaining decomposes complex tasks into focused subtasks, improving reliability and enabling per-step validation and model selection.
  • Role-based prompting shapes model behavior by assigning specific personas; multi-persona evaluation provides diverse perspectives on the same problem.
  • Meta-prompting uses LLMs to improve prompts, creating an efficient feedback loop for prompt optimization.

Practice Exercises

1. Implement a self-consistency solver for logic puzzles. Generate 7 reasoning paths at temperature 0.7, extract answers, and take the majority vote. Test it on 5 logic puzzles of increasing difficulty and measure how confidence correlates with accuracy.

2. Build a ReAct agent with at least 3 real tools: a calculator, a web search (simulated), and a file reader. Give it a multi-step research question that requires using all three tools. Log the full thought-action-observation chain and analyze the agent’s reasoning quality.

3. Create a prompt chain that takes a raw user feature request, classifies it (enhancement vs. new feature vs. bug fix), extracts requirements, generates acceptance criteria, and produces a technical design outline. Test with 5 different feature requests and evaluate the quality of each stage.

4. Use meta-prompting to iteratively improve a code review prompt. Start with a basic prompt, test it on 10 code snippets with known issues, collect the failures, use the model to suggest improvements, and repeat for 3 iterations. Document how the prompt evolves and track accuracy at each iteration.

Back to Table of Contents
Chapter 9

Function Calling and Tools

From Frontend to AI

Function calling is like server-side API routing. When a request comes in, a router inspects it and dispatches to the right handler based on the URL path and HTTP method. Similarly, when a user message comes in, the LLM inspects it and decides which function (tool) to call based on the function descriptions and the user’s intent. You define the “routes” (function schemas), the LLM acts as the “router” (deciding which function to call with what arguments), and your code serves as the “handler” (executing the actual function). The model never executes code — it only generates the function call; your application handles execution.

What Is Function Calling?

Function calling (also called tool use) is a capability that allows LLMs to request the execution of external functions during a conversation. Instead of trying to generate an answer from its training data alone, the model can recognize when it needs external capabilities — a database query, an API call, a calculation, a file operation — and emit a structured function call that your application can execute.

This is a transformational capability because it bridges the gap between what LLMs know (patterns in text) and what applications need to do (interact with the real world). Without function calling, an LLM asked “What is the current stock price of AAPL?” has to either hallucinate a number or honestly say it does not know. With function calling, it can call get_stock_price(symbol="AAPL"), receive the real-time price, and deliver an accurate answer.

Critically, the model itself never executes the function. It generates a JSON object describing which function to call and what arguments to pass. Your application code receives this JSON, executes the actual function, and sends the result back to the model, which then incorporates the result into its response. This design keeps the model sandboxed while giving it access to arbitrary capabilities.

The Function Calling Flow

Understanding the complete message flow is essential for implementing function calling correctly. The process involves multiple round trips between your application, the LLM API, and your tool implementations.

Step 1: You send the user’s message along with a list of available tool definitions to the API. Each tool definition includes a name, description, and a JSON Schema describing its parameters.

Step 2: The model analyzes the user’s message and the available tools. If it determines that a tool call is needed, it returns a response with finish_reason="tool_calls" containing one or more tool call objects with the function name and arguments.

Step 3: Your application extracts the tool calls, validates the arguments, executes each function, and collects the results.

Step 4: You send the results back to the model as tool messages, maintaining the full conversation history.

Step 5: The model processes the tool results and generates a final natural-language response for the user, or requests additional tool calls if needed.

Function Calling Sequence: Message Flow Between User, LLM, and Tools
The function calling sequence involves multiple exchanges: your application sends the user query and tool definitions to the LLM, receives a tool call request, executes the function, sends results back, and receives the final response. The LLM never executes code directly — it only generates structured tool call requests.

Defining Functions with JSON Schema

The quality of your function definitions directly determines how well the model selects and parameterizes tool calls. Each function definition consists of a name, a description, and a parameters object following JSON Schema. The description is especially important — it is the primary signal the model uses to decide whether to call a function.

Write descriptions as you would write API documentation for a colleague. Be specific about what the function does, when it should be used, what it returns, and any important constraints. Avoid vague descriptions like “gets data” — instead, write “Retrieves the current stock price and daily change for a given ticker symbol. Returns real-time market data during trading hours and last closing price outside trading hours.”

PythonDefining tools with detailed JSON Schema
tools = [
    {
        "type": "function",
        "function": {
            "name": "search_products",
            "description": (
                "Search the product catalog by keyword, category, or price range. "
                "Use when the user asks about available products, wants recommendations, "
                "or needs to find specific items. Returns up to 10 matching products "
                "sorted by relevance."
            ),
            "parameters": {
                "type": "object",
                "properties": {
                    "query": {
                        "type": "string",
                        "description": "Search keywords (e.g., 'wireless headphones', 'running shoes')"
                    },
                    "category": {
                        "type": "string",
                        "enum": ["electronics", "clothing", "home", "sports", "books"],
                        "description": "Product category to filter by"
                    },
                    "min_price": {
                        "type": "number",
                        "description": "Minimum price in USD"
                    },
                    "max_price": {
                        "type": "number",
                        "description": "Maximum price in USD"
                    },
                    "in_stock_only": {
                        "type": "boolean",
                        "description": "If true, only return products currently in stock",
                        "default": True
                    }
                },
                "required": ["query"],
                "additionalProperties": False
            }
        }
    },
    {
        "type": "function",
        "function": {
            "name": "get_order_status",
            "description": (
                "Look up the current status of a customer order by order ID. "
                "Returns shipping status, estimated delivery date, and tracking info. "
                "Use when the user asks about an order they placed."
            ),
            "parameters": {
                "type": "object",
                "properties": {
                    "order_id": {
                        "type": "string",
                        "description": "The order ID (format: ORD-XXXXX)",
                        "pattern": "^ORD-[0-9]{5}$"
                    }
                },
                "required": ["order_id"],
                "additionalProperties": False
            }
        }
    },
    {
        "type": "function",
        "function": {
            "name": "create_support_ticket",
            "description": (
                "Create a new customer support ticket. Use when the user reports "
                "a problem, requests a refund, or needs human assistance. "
                "Returns the ticket ID for reference."
            ),
            "parameters": {
                "type": "object",
                "properties": {
                    "subject": {
                        "type": "string",
                        "description": "Brief summary of the issue (max 100 chars)"
                    },
                    "description": {
                        "type": "string",
                        "description": "Detailed description of the issue"
                    },
                    "priority": {
                        "type": "string",
                        "enum": ["low", "medium", "high", "urgent"],
                        "description": "Ticket priority based on issue severity"
                    },
                    "category": {
                        "type": "string",
                        "enum": ["billing", "shipping", "product", "technical", "other"],
                        "description": "Issue category for routing"
                    }
                },
                "required": ["subject", "description", "priority", "category"],
                "additionalProperties": False
            }
        }
    }
]

Implementing the Function Calling Loop

In production, you need a robust loop that handles the back-and-forth between your application and the LLM. This loop must handle single tool calls, multiple parallel tool calls, multi-turn tool use (where the model needs several rounds of tool calls), and error cases where a tool fails.

PythonComplete function calling loop with error handling
import json
from typing import Callable

class ToolRegistry:
    """Registry for available tools and their implementations."""

    def __init__(self):
        self._tools: dict[str, dict] = {}
        self._handlers: dict[str, Callable] = {}

    def register(self, name: str, description: str, parameters: dict, handler: Callable):
        self._tools[name] = {
            "type": "function",
            "function": {
                "name": name,
                "description": description,
                "parameters": parameters
            }
        }
        self._handlers[name] = handler

    @property
    def definitions(self) -> list[dict]:
        return list(self._tools.values())

    def execute(self, name: str, arguments: dict) -> str:
        if name not in self._handlers:
            return json.dumps({"error": f"Unknown tool: {name}"})
        try:
            result = self._handlers[name](**arguments)
            return json.dumps(result) if not isinstance(result, str) else result
        except Exception as e:
            return json.dumps({"error": str(e)})


def run_tool_agent(
    client,
    registry: ToolRegistry,
    messages: list[dict],
    model: str = "gpt-4o",
    max_rounds: int = 10
) -> str:
    """Run a tool-using agent loop until completion."""

    for _ in range(max_rounds):
        response = client.chat.completions.create(
            model=model,
            messages=messages,
            tools=registry.definitions,
            tool_choice="auto"
        )

        choice = response.choices[0]
        assistant_message = choice.message

        # Add assistant message to conversation history
        messages.append(assistant_message.model_dump())

        # If no tool calls, return the final text response
        if choice.finish_reason == "stop" or not assistant_message.tool_calls:
            return assistant_message.content

        # Process each tool call (supports parallel calls)
        for tool_call in assistant_message.tool_calls:
            fn_name = tool_call.function.name
            fn_args = json.loads(tool_call.function.arguments)

            print(f"  Calling: {fn_name}({fn_args})")
            result = registry.execute(fn_name, fn_args)
            print(f"  Result: {result[:200]}")

            # Add the tool result to conversation history
            messages.append({
                "role": "tool",
                "tool_call_id": tool_call.id,
                "content": result
            })

    return "Maximum tool rounds reached without a final response."
PythonUsing the ToolRegistry to build a complete assistant
# Set up the registry with real tool implementations
registry = ToolRegistry()

registry.register(
    name="get_weather",
    description="Get current weather for a city. Use when the user asks about weather conditions.",
    parameters={
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
            "city": {"type": "string", "description": "City name"},
            "units": {
                "type": "string",
                "enum": ["celsius", "fahrenheit"],
                "default": "celsius"
            }
        },
        "required": ["city"],
        "additionalProperties": False
    },
    handler=lambda city, units="celsius": {
        "city": city,
        "temperature": 22 if units == "celsius" else 72,
        "condition": "partly cloudy",
        "humidity": 65,
        "units": units
    }
)

registry.register(
    name="calculate",
    description=(
        "Perform mathematical calculations. Use for any arithmetic, "
        "unit conversions, or mathematical operations the user requests."
    ),
    parameters={
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
            "expression": {
                "type": "string",
                "description": "Mathematical expression to evaluate (e.g., '(15 * 3) + 7')"
            }
        },
        "required": ["expression"],
        "additionalProperties": False
    },
    handler=lambda expression: {"result": eval(expression), "expression": expression}
)

registry.register(
    name="search_knowledge_base",
    description=(
        "Search the internal knowledge base for company policies, procedures, "
        "and documentation. Use when the user asks about company-specific information."
    ),
    parameters={
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
            "query": {"type": "string", "description": "Search query"},
            "department": {
                "type": "string",
                "enum": ["engineering", "hr", "finance", "legal", "all"],
                "default": "all"
            }
        },
        "required": ["query"],
        "additionalProperties": False
    },
    handler=lambda query, department="all": {
        "results": [
            {"title": f"Policy: {query}", "snippet": "Relevant policy information..."},
            {"title": f"FAQ: {query}", "snippet": "Frequently asked questions about..."}
        ],
        "total_results": 2
    }
)

# Run the agent
messages = [
    {
        "role": "system",
        "content": (
            "You are a helpful company assistant with access to weather, "
            "calculation, and knowledge base tools. Use them proactively "
            "when they can help answer the user's question. Be concise."
        )
    },
    {
        "role": "user",
        "content": (
            "I need to plan an outdoor team event. What's the weather in "
            "San Francisco? Also, if we have 23 people and lunch costs $18.50 "
            "per person, what's the total cost including 8.625% tax?"
        )
    }
]

# The agent will make parallel tool calls:
# 1. get_weather(city="San Francisco")
# 2. calculate(expression="23 * 18.50 * 1.08625")
answer = run_tool_agent(client, registry, messages)
print(answer)

Parallel Function Calling

Modern LLM APIs support parallel function calling, where the model can request multiple tool calls in a single response. This is important for both performance and user experience — if the user asks a question that requires data from multiple sources, the model can request all of them simultaneously rather than sequentially.

In the example above, the model recognizes that the weather lookup and the cost calculation are independent operations and requests both in a single response. Your application should execute these in parallel (e.g., using asyncio.gather) for optimal latency.

PythonHandling parallel tool calls with async execution
import asyncio

async def execute_tools_parallel(
    registry: ToolRegistry,
    tool_calls: list
) -> list[dict]:
    """Execute multiple tool calls in parallel."""

    async def run_one(tool_call):
        fn_name = tool_call.function.name
        fn_args = json.loads(tool_call.function.arguments)
        result = registry.execute(fn_name, fn_args)
        return {
            "role": "tool",
            "tool_call_id": tool_call.id,
            "content": result
        }

    results = await asyncio.gather(
        *(run_one(tc) for tc in tool_calls)
    )
    return list(results)


async def run_tool_agent_async(
    client,
    registry: ToolRegistry,
    messages: list[dict],
    model: str = "gpt-4o",
    max_rounds: int = 10
) -> str:
    """Async tool agent with parallel execution support."""

    for _ in range(max_rounds):
        response = client.chat.completions.create(
            model=model,
            messages=messages,
            tools=registry.definitions,
            tool_choice="auto"
        )

        choice = response.choices[0]
        messages.append(choice.message.model_dump())

        if choice.finish_reason == "stop" or not choice.message.tool_calls:
            return choice.message.content

        # Execute all tool calls in parallel
        tool_results = await execute_tools_parallel(
            registry, choice.message.tool_calls
        )
        messages.extend(tool_results)

    return "Max rounds reached."

Structured Outputs with Function Calling

One of the most practical applications of function calling is not actually calling external functions — it is using the function calling mechanism to force the model to return structured data. By defining a “function” whose parameters match the JSON schema you want, and setting tool_choice to force that function, you get reliably structured outputs.

OpenAI has formalized this pattern with their Structured Outputs feature, which guarantees that the model’s output conforms exactly to your specified JSON Schema. This is achieved through constrained decoding — at each token generation step, the model is restricted to only tokens that maintain a valid partial JSON according to your schema.

PythonStructured outputs for reliable data extraction
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from typing import Optional

class Address(BaseModel):
    street: str
    city: str
    state: str
    zip_code: str
    country: str = "US"

class ContactInfo(BaseModel):
    name: str = Field(description="Full name of the person")
    email: Optional[str] = Field(None, description="Email address if mentioned")
    phone: Optional[str] = Field(None, description="Phone number if mentioned")
    company: Optional[str] = Field(None, description="Company name if mentioned")
    role: Optional[str] = Field(None, description="Job title or role if mentioned")
    address: Optional[Address] = Field(None, description="Physical address if mentioned")

class ExtractionResult(BaseModel):
    contacts: list[ContactInfo]
    confidence: float = Field(description="Confidence score from 0.0 to 1.0")
    notes: str = Field(description="Any additional observations about the text")

# Use structured outputs for guaranteed schema compliance
response = client.beta.chat.completions.parse(
    model="gpt-4o",
    messages=[
        {
            "role": "system",
            "content": (
                "Extract contact information from text. "
                "Be thorough but only include information explicitly stated. "
                "Rate your confidence based on how clear the information is."
            )
        },
        {
            "role": "user",
            "content": (
                "Hi, I'm reaching out on behalf of our CTO, Marcus Williams. "
                "He can be reached at marcus@techcorp.io or (555) 234-5678. "
                "Our office is at 456 Innovation Drive, Austin, TX 78701. "
                "Also, please CC our project lead, Priya Patel, at priya@techcorp.io."
            )
        }
    ],
    response_format=ExtractionResult
)

result = response.choices[0].message.parsed
for contact in result.contacts:
    print(f"{contact.name} ({contact.role or 'No role'}) - {contact.email}")

Tool Orchestration Patterns

As your applications grow more complex, you will need to think carefully about how tools interact. There are three primary orchestration patterns.

Sequential: Tool calls happen one after another, where each call depends on the result of the previous one. Example: look up a customer ID, then use that ID to fetch their order history, then use the order data to generate a summary. The model naturally handles this by making one tool call per round.

Parallel: Multiple independent tool calls happen simultaneously. Example: fetch weather data, stock prices, and news headlines all at once to generate a morning briefing. The model requests all three in a single response.

Conditional: The choice of which tool to call depends on the result of a previous tool call or on intermediate reasoning. Example: if the customer’s account is delinquent (determined by a lookup), route to a billing tool; otherwise, proceed with their original request. The model handles this through its natural reasoning capabilities.

Pattern When to Use Latency Example
Sequential Each step depends on the previous result High (multiple round trips) Look up user → fetch orders → summarize
Parallel Multiple independent data sources needed Low (single round trip) Fetch weather + stocks + news simultaneously
Conditional Next action depends on intermediate results Variable Check status → escalate OR resolve

The tool_choice Parameter

The tool_choice parameter controls whether and how the model uses tools. Understanding its options gives you fine-grained control over the model’s behavior.

"auto" (default): The model decides whether to call a tool or respond directly. This is the most common setting and works well in most cases.

"none": The model is prohibited from calling any tools and must respond with text only. Use this when you want to force a conversational response even when tools are available.

"required": The model must call at least one tool. Use this when you know a tool call is necessary — for example, when the user’s request can only be fulfilled by querying a database.

{"type": "function", "function": {"name": "specific_function"}}: Forces the model to call a specific function. Use this for structured output extraction or when you have already determined which tool is needed through your application logic.

PythonUsing tool_choice for different scenarios
# Auto mode - model decides (default for general assistants)
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o",
    messages=messages,
    tools=tools,
    tool_choice="auto"
)

# Required mode - force a tool call (when you know external data is needed)
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o",
    messages=[
        {"role": "system", "content": "Look up real-time data to answer questions."},
        {"role": "user", "content": "What is NVIDIA's current stock price?"}
    ],
    tools=tools,
    tool_choice="required"
)

# Forced specific function - guaranteed structured output
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o",
    messages=[
        {"role": "user", "content": "Extract entities from: 'Apple CEO Tim Cook announced...'"}
    ],
    tools=[{
        "type": "function",
        "function": {
            "name": "extract_entities",
            "description": "Extract named entities from text",
            "parameters": {
                "type": "object",
                "properties": {
                    "persons": {"type": "array", "items": {"type": "string"}},
                    "organizations": {"type": "array", "items": {"type": "string"}},
                    "locations": {"type": "array", "items": {"type": "string"}}
                },
                "required": ["persons", "organizations", "locations"],
                "additionalProperties": False
            }
        }
    }],
    tool_choice={"type": "function", "function": {"name": "extract_entities"}}
)

Real-World Use Cases

Function calling enables a wide range of practical applications. Here are the most common patterns you will encounter in production.

Database queries: The model translates natural language questions into SQL queries, your code executes them against the database, and the model formats the results as a human-readable answer. This is one of the most impactful use cases, turning any database into a conversational interface.

API integrations: The model acts as a natural-language interface to third-party APIs — Slack, Jira, GitHub, Salesforce, and others. Instead of navigating complex UIs, users describe what they want in plain English, and the model generates the appropriate API calls.

Calculations and data analysis: While LLMs can do basic arithmetic, they are unreliable for complex calculations. By providing a calculator tool, you get accurate math with natural-language input and output.

File and document operations: Reading, writing, transforming, and summarizing files. The model decides what operations are needed and generates the appropriate calls.

Multi-system workflows: Complex operations that span multiple systems — for example, creating a new user account might require calls to an auth service, a database, an email system, and an analytics platform. The model orchestrates the entire sequence.

PythonReal-world example: natural language database query assistant
import sqlite3

def create_db_assistant(db_path: str):
    """Create a natural-language database query assistant."""

    conn = sqlite3.connect(db_path)

    def get_table_schemas() -> dict:
        cursor = conn.execute(
            "SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table'"
        )
        schemas = {}
        for (table_name,) in cursor.fetchall():
            col_cursor = conn.execute(f"PRAGMA table_info({table_name})")
            columns = [
                {"name": row[1], "type": row[2], "nullable": not row[3]}
                for row in col_cursor.fetchall()
            ]
            schemas[table_name] = columns
        return schemas

    def run_sql_query(query: str) -> dict:
        try:
            cursor = conn.execute(query)
            columns = [desc[0] for desc in cursor.description] if cursor.description else []
            rows = cursor.fetchall()
            return {
                "columns": columns,
                "rows": [dict(zip(columns, row)) for row in rows[:50]],
                "total_rows": len(rows),
                "truncated": len(rows) > 50
            }
        except Exception as e:
            return {"error": str(e)}

    schemas = get_table_schemas()
    schema_description = "\n".join(
        f"Table '{name}': {', '.join(c['name'] + ' (' + c['type'] + ')' for c in cols)}"
        for name, cols in schemas.items()
    )

    registry = ToolRegistry()
    registry.register(
        name="run_sql",
        description=(
            "Execute a read-only SQL query against the database. "
            "Only SELECT queries are permitted. "
            f"Available tables and columns:\n{schema_description}"
        ),
        parameters={
            "type": "object",
            "properties": {
                "query": {
                    "type": "string",
                    "description": "SQL SELECT query to execute"
                },
                "explanation": {
                    "type": "string",
                    "description": "Brief explanation of what this query does"
                }
            },
            "required": ["query", "explanation"],
            "additionalProperties": False
        },
        handler=lambda query, explanation="": run_sql_query(query)
    )

    return registry


# Usage
db_registry = create_db_assistant("company.db")
messages = [
    {
        "role": "system",
        "content": (
            "You are a data analyst assistant. Answer questions by querying "
            "the database. Always explain your query logic before executing. "
            "Present results in a clear, readable format."
        )
    },
    {
        "role": "user",
        "content": "What were our top 5 products by revenue last quarter?"
    }
]

answer = run_tool_agent(client, db_registry, messages)
print(answer)

Best Practices for Function Definitions

After building many tool-using applications, several best practices emerge.

Write descriptions for the model, not just for humans. The description is the model’s primary guide for deciding when to use a tool. Include what the function does, when it should be used, what it returns, and common use cases. A well-described function dramatically reduces misuse.

Use enums and constraints liberally. Instead of accepting free-form strings for parameters that have a fixed set of values, use enums. Instead of accepting any number for a parameter that has valid ranges, use minimum and maximum constraints. Tight schemas produce more reliable tool calls.

Keep the number of tools manageable. While models can handle dozens of tools, accuracy decreases as the number grows. If you have more than 10-15 tools, consider grouping them into categories and using a two-stage approach: first select the category, then present only the relevant tools.

Design for failure. Tools will fail — APIs time out, databases go down, inputs are invalid. Always return structured error messages from your tool implementations so the model can inform the user gracefully or try an alternative approach.

Validate arguments before execution. Even though the model generates arguments based on your schema, always validate them in your code before executing the function. This is especially important for tools that modify data or interact with external systems.

Include parameter descriptions. Every parameter should have a description that explains what values are expected and gives examples. The model uses these descriptions to generate appropriate arguments.

Key Concept

Function calling transforms LLMs from text generators into orchestrators. The model becomes a reasoning layer that decides what actions to take and in what order, while your code handles the actual execution. This separation of concerns — the model reasons, your code acts — is a fundamental architectural pattern in AI engineering. It keeps the model sandboxed, gives you full control over side effects, and allows you to swap, test, and monitor tools independently.

Pro Tip

When developing tool-using applications, start by running the model with tool definitions but without executing any tools — just log what the model would call and with what arguments. This “dry run” approach lets you verify that the model selects the right tools and generates correct arguments before you invest in building tool execution infrastructure. It is the AI equivalent of mocking your API integrations during development.

Security Considerations

Function calling introduces unique security concerns that you must address in production applications.

Prompt injection through tool results: If a tool returns user-generated content (e.g., from a database or web page), that content could contain instructions that the model follows. Always sanitize tool outputs and consider wrapping them in XML tags to delineate data from instructions.

Overly permissive tools: Avoid giving the model access to tools that can perform destructive operations (DELETE, DROP TABLE, etc.) without human confirmation. Implement a confirmation step for high-risk actions.

Argument injection: If tool arguments are interpolated into SQL queries or shell commands, you face the classic injection risk. Always use parameterized queries and never pass model-generated strings directly to command execution.

Rate limiting and cost control: A model in a tool-calling loop can potentially make many API calls in rapid succession. Implement rate limiting on tool execution, set maximum round limits on the agent loop, and monitor costs carefully.

PythonSecurity-hardened tool execution
class SecureToolRegistry(ToolRegistry):
    """Tool registry with security guardrails."""

    HIGH_RISK_TOOLS = {"delete_record", "update_record", "send_email", "run_sql_write"}

    def __init__(self, require_confirmation: bool = True):
        super().__init__()
        self.require_confirmation = require_confirmation
        self._call_count = 0
        self._max_calls_per_session = 50

    def execute(self, name: str, arguments: dict) -> str:
        self._call_count += 1
        if self._call_count > self._max_calls_per_session:
            return json.dumps({
                "error": "Maximum tool calls per session exceeded. "
                         "Please start a new conversation."
            })

        # Require human confirmation for high-risk tools
        if self.require_confirmation and name in self.HIGH_RISK_TOOLS:
            return json.dumps({
                "status": "confirmation_required",
                "message": f"Action '{name}' requires user confirmation.",
                "details": arguments
            })

        # Sanitize string arguments to prevent injection
        sanitized_args = {}
        for key, value in arguments.items():
            if isinstance(value, str):
                sanitized_args[key] = value.replace("'", "''")
            else:
                sanitized_args[key] = value

        return super().execute(name, sanitized_args)

Key Takeaways

  • Function calling enables LLMs to interact with external systems by generating structured tool call requests that your code executes — the model reasons, your code acts.
  • High-quality function definitions with detailed descriptions, constrained parameters, and clear schemas are the foundation of reliable tool use.
  • The function calling loop involves multiple round trips: send tools and messages, receive tool call requests, execute functions, return results, and repeat until the model produces a final response.
  • Parallel function calling improves latency by allowing the model to request multiple independent tool calls in a single response.
  • Structured outputs (via function calling or dedicated APIs) guarantee that model responses conform to a specific JSON Schema, eliminating parsing errors.
  • Use tool_choice to control when and how the model uses tools: "auto" for general use, "required" when a tool call is necessary, or force a specific function for structured extraction.
  • Security is critical: validate arguments, sanitize tool outputs, implement confirmation steps for destructive operations, and set rate limits on tool execution.

Practice Exercises

1. Build a complete tool-using assistant with at least 4 tools: a weather API, a calculator, a to-do list manager (with add, list, delete operations), and a unit converter. Implement the full function calling loop with error handling and test with multi-tool queries.

2. Create a natural-language SQL assistant for a sample SQLite database (create a database with users, orders, and products tables with sample data). The assistant should be able to answer questions like “Which customers ordered more than 3 times last month?” Implement proper SQL injection protection.

3. Implement a SecureToolRegistry that includes rate limiting (max N calls per minute), argument validation against the JSON Schema before execution, logging of all tool calls and results, and a confirmation workflow for high-risk tools. Write tests for each security feature.

4. Build a multi-step workflow assistant that can handle tasks like “Create a Jira ticket for bug X, assign it to the on-call engineer, and send a Slack notification to the team channel.” Simulate the external APIs with mock functions, but implement the full orchestration logic with proper error handling for partial failures.

Back to Table of Contents
Part IV

Embeddings and Vector Databases

Understanding how AI represents meaning as numbers, and the specialized databases that make semantic search, recommendations, and retrieval-augmented generation possible.

Chapter 10

Understanding Embeddings

From Frontend to AI

If you have used full-text search in Elasticsearch, embeddings are the next evolution. Instead of matching keywords, embeddings capture meaning. “Happy” and “joyful” would be far apart in keyword search but close together in embedding space. Think of it like upgrading from exact CSS color matching (#FF0000) to understanding that “crimson,” “scarlet,” and “ruby” all refer to shades of red.

What Are Embeddings?

At their core, embeddings are dense vector representations of data—lists of floating-point numbers that encode the meaning of a piece of text, an image, a code snippet, or virtually any structured or unstructured data. Unlike sparse representations such as one-hot encoding or TF-IDF (where vectors contain mostly zeros), embeddings pack information into every dimension, producing compact and information-rich numerical fingerprints.

Consider the sentence “The cat sat on the mat.” A traditional bag-of-words model would represent this as a sparse vector with ones at positions corresponding to each word in a vocabulary of tens of thousands of entries. An embedding model, by contrast, compresses the entire sentence into a dense vector of, say, 384 or 1536 floating-point numbers. Each number contributes to encoding various semantic properties: topic, sentiment, formality, specificity, and countless other latent features that the model learned during training.

The critical property of embeddings is that semantically similar inputs produce numerically similar vectors. The sentence “A feline rested on the rug” should produce a vector very close to our original sentence, even though only one word (“the”) is shared between them. This is what makes embeddings so powerful for search, recommendation, and retrieval tasks.

Key Concept

Embeddings are learned representations. The model does not follow hand-coded rules to assign numbers—it learns, through exposure to billions of text examples, which numerical patterns best capture semantic relationships. This is why embedding quality depends heavily on the training data and objective of the model that produced them.

How Embedding Models Work

Modern text embedding models are built on the Transformer encoder architecture—the same foundational technology behind BERT and its descendants. Here is a simplified overview of the pipeline:

  1. Tokenization: The input text is split into subword tokens. For example, “embeddings” might become ["embed", "##dings"]. This allows the model to handle words it has never seen before by decomposing them into familiar subparts.
  2. Token Embedding Lookup: Each token is mapped to an initial dense vector from a learned vocabulary table. At this stage, the vectors have no contextual information—“bank” has the same vector whether it refers to a river bank or a financial institution.
  3. Transformer Encoder Layers: The token vectors pass through multiple self-attention layers. This is where the magic happens: each token “attends” to every other token, allowing the model to build context-aware representations. After these layers, the vector for “bank” in “river bank” differs from “bank” in “bank account.”
  4. Pooling: The model now has one vector per token, but we need a single vector for the entire input. Common strategies include mean pooling (averaging all token vectors), CLS token pooling (using the special classification token’s vector), or max pooling. Mean pooling is the most common in modern sentence embedding models.
  5. Normalization: The final vector is typically L2-normalized so that its magnitude is 1. This ensures that cosine similarity reduces to a simple dot product, making distance computations faster.

The model is then fine-tuned with a contrastive learning objective: given pairs of semantically similar texts (positive pairs) and dissimilar texts (negative pairs), the model learns to push similar pairs closer together in vector space and pull dissimilar pairs apart. This training process is what gives embeddings their remarkable ability to capture semantic relationships.

Dimensionality: What 384, 768, and 1536 Dimensions Mean

When you see that an embedding model produces 384-dimensional vectors, it means each piece of text is represented as a list of 384 floating-point numbers. But what do these dimensions represent?

Unlike hand-engineered features (where dimension 1 might be “sentiment” and dimension 2 might be “formality”), embedding dimensions do not have clean, human-interpretable meanings. Each dimension encodes a combination of learned features. However, collectively, these dimensions form a rich representational space where proximity encodes similarity.

The practical implications of dimensionality are significant:

  • 384 dimensions (e.g., all-MiniLM-L6-v2): Compact and fast. Each vector uses about 1.5 KB of memory (384 × 4 bytes for float32). Suitable for applications where speed and memory efficiency matter more than maximum accuracy. A million documents would require roughly 1.5 GB of vector storage.
  • 768 dimensions (e.g., BGE-base, E5-base): The standard size for BERT-based models. Offers a good balance of performance and efficiency. A million documents need about 3 GB of vector storage.
  • 1536 dimensions (e.g., OpenAI text-embedding-3-small): Higher capacity for nuanced semantic distinctions. A million documents need about 6 GB. Best for applications requiring maximum retrieval accuracy.
  • 3072 dimensions (e.g., OpenAI text-embedding-3-large): Top-tier accuracy for the most demanding use cases. A million documents need about 12 GB. Often overkill for most applications.

A useful rule of thumb for frontend developers: choosing embedding dimensionality is analogous to choosing image resolution. A 384-dimension embedding is like a thumbnail—fast to load and usually sufficient for browsing. A 3072-dimension embedding is like a 4K image—captures fine details but costs more to store and process. The right choice depends on your accuracy requirements and infrastructure constraints.

Key Concept

OpenAI’s text-embedding-3 models support a dimensions parameter that lets you truncate the output vector to a smaller size (e.g., request only 256 dimensions from a 1536-dimensional model). This uses Matryoshka Representation Learning, where the most important information is packed into the first dimensions. This gives you a flexible accuracy-vs-cost tradeoff without switching models.

Semantic Similarity and Cosine Similarity

The entire point of embeddings is to measure how similar two pieces of content are. The most widely used metric for this is cosine similarity, which measures the angle between two vectors regardless of their magnitude.

Cosine similarity ranges from −1 (completely opposite) through 0 (unrelated) to 1 (identical meaning). In practice, with normalized embeddings, you will rarely see values below 0. Most similar pairs fall in the 0.7–0.95 range, while unrelated pairs hover around 0.1–0.4.

The formula is straightforward:

cosine_similarity(A, B) = (A · B) / (||A|| × ||B||)

Where A · B is the dot product and ||A|| is the magnitude (L2 norm) of vector A. When both vectors are pre-normalized to unit length (which most embedding models do), the formula simplifies to just the dot product.

Other distance metrics you may encounter include:

  • Euclidean distance (L2): Measures straight-line distance in vector space. Lower values mean more similar. Common but less popular than cosine similarity for text embeddings.
  • Dot product: Equivalent to cosine similarity when vectors are normalized. Some vector databases use this as their default metric for performance reasons.
  • Manhattan distance (L1): Sum of absolute differences. Rarely used for embeddings but occasionally seen in specialized applications.
PythonComputing Cosine Similarity from Scratch
import numpy as np

def cosine_similarity(vec_a, vec_b):
    """Compute cosine similarity between two vectors."""
    dot_product = np.dot(vec_a, vec_b)
    norm_a = np.linalg.norm(vec_a)
    norm_b = np.linalg.norm(vec_b)
    return dot_product / (norm_a * norm_b)

# Example: similar sentences produce high similarity
embedding_happy = np.array([0.2, 0.8, 0.1, 0.5])   # simplified
embedding_joyful = np.array([0.25, 0.78, 0.12, 0.48])
embedding_car = np.array([0.9, 0.1, 0.7, 0.05])

print(cosine_similarity(embedding_happy, embedding_joyful))  # ~0.999 (very similar)
print(cosine_similarity(embedding_happy, embedding_car))      # ~0.467 (unrelated)

OpenAI Embedding Models

OpenAI provides some of the most popular commercial embedding models, favored for their quality, ease of use, and the fact that they require zero infrastructure to run.

text-embedding-3-small is the workhorse model for most applications. It produces 1536-dimensional vectors by default (truncatable to as low as 256), offers strong performance across benchmarks, and costs $0.02 per million tokens—making it extremely affordable at scale. For most production applications, this is the model to start with.

text-embedding-3-large is the premium option, producing 3072-dimensional vectors by default. It achieves higher scores on retrieval benchmarks, particularly for nuanced queries or domain-specific content. At $0.13 per million tokens, it is still inexpensive but 6.5x the cost of the small model. Use it when retrieval quality is paramount.

PythonGenerating Embeddings with OpenAI
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()

def get_embedding(text, model="text-embedding-3-small", dimensions=None):
    """Generate an embedding for a piece of text using OpenAI."""
    params = {"input": text, "model": model}
    if dimensions:
        params["dimensions"] = dimensions
    response = client.embeddings.create(**params)
    return response.data[0].embedding

# Generate a standard 1536-d embedding
embedding = get_embedding("Retrieval-augmented generation improves LLM accuracy")
print(f"Dimensions: {len(embedding)}")  # 1536
print(f"First 5 values: {embedding[:5]}")

# Generate a compact 256-d embedding (Matryoshka truncation)
compact = get_embedding(
    "Retrieval-augmented generation improves LLM accuracy",
    dimensions=256
)
print(f"Compact dimensions: {len(compact)}")  # 256

# Batch embedding for efficiency
def get_embeddings_batch(texts, model="text-embedding-3-small"):
    """Embed multiple texts in a single API call (up to 2048 inputs)."""
    response = client.embeddings.create(input=texts, model=model)
    return [item.embedding for item in response.data]

documents = [
    "Python is a programming language",
    "JavaScript runs in the browser",
    "Transformers revolutionized NLP",
]
embeddings = get_embeddings_batch(documents)
print(f"Generated {len(embeddings)} embeddings")

Sentence Transformers: Open-Source Embedding Models

While OpenAI embeddings are convenient, there are compelling reasons to use open-source models: cost (free to run locally), privacy (data never leaves your infrastructure), latency (no network round-trips), and customizability (you can fine-tune them on your domain). The Sentence Transformers library is the gold standard for open-source text embeddings.

Built on top of Hugging Face Transformers, Sentence Transformers provides pre-trained models specifically optimized for producing meaningful sentence and paragraph embeddings. The library handles all the complexity of tokenization, encoding, pooling, and normalization behind a simple API.

PythonGenerating Embeddings with Sentence Transformers
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer

# Load a pre-trained model (downloads on first use, ~80MB)
model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")

# Encode single texts
sentences = [
    "The weather is beautiful today",
    "It's a gorgeous day outside",
    "Stock prices fell sharply yesterday",
]
embeddings = model.encode(sentences)

print(f"Shape: {embeddings.shape}")  # (3, 384)

# Compute similarity between all pairs
from sentence_transformers.util import cos_sim
